title: Modern Leaf-Shaped Plant Stand / Magazine Rack
summary: A modern leaf-shaped magazine rack and plant stand combo, cut from one oak plywood panel and standing on angled dowel legs.
description:
This piece does double duty by design — the same angled-panel shape works equally well leaning magazines against as it does holding a potted plant, which is exactly the kind of multi-use furniture that's worth the extra layout work up front.

The legs are **dowels drilled in at an angle**, not vertical — an angled drill attachment holds the bit at a consistent splay while you drill, which is what gives the stand its confident, mid-century-modern stance instead of looking like a flat panel awkwardly balanced on straight sticks.

Cut the panel to your own leaf or geometric shape using a template; the dowel joinery and angled-leg technique are what actually make this project work, and they don't care what outline you trace.

cutList.note: Cut to your chosen leaf or geometric shape using a template.

steps.title: Cut the panel to shape
steps.body: Start from a 3/4" oak plywood blank 16" x 24", then lay your leaf or geometric outline onto it with a paper template so both halves match and you can move the shape around before committing. Keep the outline to straight cuts if the table saw is the only saw you have — a table saw cannot cut a curve, and the joinery in this project is what makes it work, not the outline. If you want a true leaf shape, cut it with a jigsaw or band saw and clean the edge with sandpaper wrapped around a block. Whatever shape you choose, leave enough solid material at the four leg positions to take a 1-1/4" hole without breaking out at an edge.

steps.title: Drill the angled leg holes
steps.body: Mark the four leg positions on the panel and drill each with a 1-1/4" forstner bit to match the leg dowels. Set an angled drill attachment so the bit leans out consistently — the splay is what gives this piece its stance, and four legs at four slightly different angles will rock. Test the setup on an offcut of the same 3/4" plywood first, and check that the sample dowel stands the way you want before you touch the real panel. Because the panel is only 3/4" thick and the holes are 1-1/4" across, these come out as through-holes; back the panel with scrap so the bit does not blow out the underside, and clamp the panel down rather than holding it.

steps.title: Check the dowel lengths
steps.body: The dowels are sold at their finished lengths, so there is usually nothing to cut: the four 1-1/4" leg dowels come at 32", the two 1" short braces at 18", and the 3/4" long brace at 32". Because the legs splay, the stand will sit lower than 32" tall and the feet will need to be trimmed square to the floor once the legs are in — stand the assembly up on a flat surface, scribe a line around each foot parallel to the floor, and cut to it. Do that after assembly, not before, since the trim depends on the splay you actually drilled.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.body: Glue the four leg dowels into their angled holes, seating each one fully and wiping the squeeze-out before it dries — end-grain-to-plywood glue joints are stronger than they look, but only if the dowel is fully home. With the legs set, hold the two 18" short braces across each pair of legs and the 32" long brace between the pairs, mark them where they actually land against the splayed legs, and fasten with pocket screws and glue at each contact point. Bracing a splayed set of legs is what stops the stand from racking sideways under a loaded pot, so fit each brace tight rather than close.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand the panel faces and the sawn outline smooth, and pay particular attention to the plywood edge, which is the one surface that shows the piece is plywood — sand it fine and it takes finish far better. Ease the sharp corners of the outline and the cut ends of the legs. Finish as desired; oak plywood takes a clear finish well, and a stand meant to carry a potted plant is worth sealing on the top face against water.
